title: The Unelected Infrastructure
synopsis: Cambridge Analytica is the scandal. What lies beneath it is the revelation, and the revelation is bigger than the scandal. The scandal is that a po...
preview: Cambridge Analytica is the scandal. What lies beneath it is the revelation, and the revelation is bigger than the scandal. The scandal is that a po...
content:
Cambridge Analytica is the scandal. What lies beneath it is the revelation, and the revelation is bigger than the scandal.

The scandal is that a political consultancy harvested data from millions of Facebook users without meaningful consent and used it to target political messaging during elections. This is bad. People are right to be angry. Regulations should be tightened. Accountability should follow.

But the scandal is a surface disturbance above a much deeper current, and I worry that the outrage about Cambridge Analytica will exhaust the public's attention before it reaches the thing that actually matters.

The thing that actually matters is this: public cognition now runs on private infrastructure. The way people encounter information, form opinions, discuss ideas, and make decisions about their collective future, all of this now happens inside systems that are privately owned, algorithmically managed, and accountable to no democratic process of any kind.

Nobody elected Facebook to organise public discourse. Nobody voted for Google to determine what information is discoverable and what is buried. Nobody chose Twitter as the medium through which political conversation would be conducted. These platforms became the infrastructure of public life not through any deliberate social decision but through the accumulated force of convenience, network effects, and the simple fact that they are good products that people wanted to use.

The result is that the most important infrastructure in any democracy, the infrastructure through which citizens inform themselves and communicate with each other, is controlled by entities whose incentives are entirely commercial. They optimise for engagement, which is to say they optimise for the emotional states that keep people on the platform: outrage, anxiety, tribal identification, the dopamine loop of notification and response.

The dynamic is an optimisation function, nothing more. The algorithm does not want you angry. It does not want anything. It simply promotes content that generates engagement, and engagement correlates with emotional arousal, and emotional arousal correlates with exactly the psychological states that are least conducive to thoughtful democratic deliberation.

The Cambridge Analytica scandal is treated as an aberration. A bad actor exploiting a system that would otherwise be benign. I think this is exactly wrong. Cambridge Analytica was not an aberration. It was an inevitability. When you build infrastructure that can model human psychology at scale and make that infrastructure available to anyone with a budget, someone will use it for political manipulation. The surprise is not that it happened but that anyone was surprised.

I keep returning to a historical analogy that I think is underappreciated. In the nineteenth century, the railroad transformed the physical infrastructure of civilisation. It connected markets, moved goods, enabled the growth of cities. It also concentrated enormous power in the hands of the companies that owned the rails. The railroad barons became, for a time, more powerful than the governments that nominally regulated them. They set prices, determined routes, decided which towns would prosper and which would wither. The democratic response took decades to develop: antitrust law, the Interstate Commerce Commission, the eventual recognition that infrastructure this important could not be left entirely to private control.

We are in the railroad baron phase of digital infrastructure. The platforms are more powerful than the governments that attempt to regulate them. They have more technical expertise, more data, more capital, and more influence over the daily lives of citizens than any public institution. The regulatory response is in its infancy, and the regulators are outmatched.

From Australia, the picture is particularly stark. We are not merely failing to regulate the platforms effectively. We have no domestic platforms to regulate. The infrastructure of Australian public life is entirely American. The servers are in Virginia and Oregon. The algorithms are written in California. The corporate decisions that determine what Australians see, read, and discuss are made in board rooms where no Australian sits.

This is not something that happened to us. We chose it, in the same way that we chose to let manufacturing decline: by not choosing otherwise. By integrating enthusiastically into systems built elsewhere, by failing to invest in domestic capability, by treating digital infrastructure as a commercial product rather than a strategic asset.

The Cambridge Analytica scandal will fade. There will be hearings, perhaps fines, possibly regulations. The platforms will adjust. The news cycle will move on.

The underlying reality will not change. The infrastructure of public thought will remain privately owned and algorithmically managed. The optimisation for engagement will continue. The asymmetry between the platforms' understanding of citizens and citizens' understanding of the platforms will continue to grow.

And the question that nobody in a position of authority seems willing to ask will remain unanswered: is self-governance possible when the infrastructure of public cognition is controlled by entities that the democratic process cannot reach?

I do not know the answer. I am increasingly concerned that the answer is no.
